coredata.dc:title: Controllable release ratiometric fluorescent sensor for hyaluronidase via the combination of Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes and degradable intelligent hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xuemin
Liu, Qingfeng
Wu, Cuimin
Lin, Zhenyu
Huang, Aiwen
Qiu, Bin
coredata.dc:description: As a popular controllable-released carrier, intelligent hydrogels are often used in drug delivery and disease therapeutics. Meanwhile, benefit from the mimic-enzyme activity performance, Fe–N–C nanozymes have been widely used in sensing and analysis. However, the combination of intelligent hydrogels with specific degradability and Fe–N–C nanozymes with enhanced activity in one system to achieve controllable and sensitive detection is rare. Herein, we combine intelligent hydrogel with mimic peroxidase activity enhanced Fe–N–C nanozymes to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensitive detection of hyaluronidase (HAase). The modification of copper ions has been proved to enhance the mimic enzyme activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes greatly. Cu2+ modified Fe–N–C nanozymes were embedded in hyaluronic acid hydrogel. In the presence of HAase, the HA hydrogel structure was hydrolyzed and released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes gradually. The released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes are used to catalyze the hydrogen peroxide system so that o-phenylenediamine is oxidized to orange fluorescent 2, 3-diaminophenolazine (DAP). Due to the electrostatic interaction, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer can occur between the negatively charged copper nanoclusters emitted by 430 nm and the positively charged DAP emitted by 560 nm. The activity of HAase was monitored according to the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 560 nm and 430 nm (F560/F430). The linear range of this method is 0–10.0 U/ml and the detection limit is 0.43 U/mL (S/N = 3). This strategy has been further applied to biological samples successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intelligent hydrogels
Nanozymes
Ratiometric fluorescence
Metal nanoclusters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhancement of peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes
The FRET process of the purposed sensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Nature Sciences Funding of Fujian Province
STS Key Project of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of STS Key Project of Fujian Province ( 2017T3007 ), Nature Sciences Funding of Fujian Province ( 2018J01682 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of PVP-CuNCs; (A) TEM image of PVP-CuNCs. (B) UV absorption (dotted line) and fluorescence spectra (solid line) of PVP-CuNCs (50.0 μL). (C) Emission spectra with different excitation wavelength of PVP-CuNCs (50.0 μL). (D) IR spectra of PVP and PVP-CuNCs.

Characterization of Fe–N–C nanozymes and Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes; (A) TEM image of Fe–N–C nanozymes. (B) HAADF-STEM image and EDS mapping images of C, N, and Fe. (C) XPS spectrum in the N 1s region of Fe–N–C nanozymes. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of H2O2/Fe–N–C nanozyme catalytic system with substrates of OPD, TMB, PPD and ABTS, respectively. Inset are the corresponding photos under visible light. The concentration of allochroic substrate (OPD, TMB, PPD and ABTS) was 10.0 mM. (E) Fluorescence spectrum of H2O2 catalytic system with Fe–N–C nanozymes and Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes. Inset are the color change of OPD after oxidation (F) Absorption spots of blank (1 M H2O2+10.0 mM OPD) with 20.0 mg/mL of Fe–N–C nanozymes and Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The FRET process of this platform; (A) Absorption spectra of DAP and OPD, and fluorescence emission spectra of PVP-CuNCs. (B) Zeta potentials of PVP-CuNCs and PVP-CuNCs + DAP. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of PVP-CuNCs, DAP and PVP-CuNCs + DAP, respectively. (D) Fluorescence responses of sensing platform upon addition of various concentrations of OPD.
Time optimization of the purposed sensing platform; (A) Time optimization of hydrolysis of hyaluronic acid hydrogel; (B) Time optimization of Fe–N–C nanozymes-catalyzed hydrogen peroxide system.

Quantitative assays of HAase; (A) Fluorescence responses of sensing platform upon addition of various activities of HAase. (B) Plots of F560/F430 versus HAase activity from 0 to 50.0 U/mL (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 0 to 10.0 U/mL).

Effect of interferents on this platform; Fluorescence sensing system responses to various species: GOx (10.0 μg/mL), LYS (10.0 μg/mL), HSA (10.0 μg/mL), β-Gal (5.0 U/L), ALP (5.0 U/L), KCl (10.0 mM), NaCl (10.0 mM). Results are the mean ± standard deviation of three separate measurements. F0 is the F560/F430 with other interference, while F is the F560/F430 with pure HAase.
Schematic representation of the strategy for the determination of HAase.
Comparison of different methods for HAase determination.
Determination of HAase in urine samples by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Controllable release ratiometric fluorescent sensor for hyaluronidase via the combination of Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes and degradable intelligent hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety, Eel Farming and Processing, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, PR China

Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, China.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a popular controllable-released carrier, intelligent hydrogels are often used in drug delivery and disease therapeutics. Meanwhile, benefit from the mimic-enzyme activity performance, Fe–N–C nanozymes have been widely used in sensing and analysis. However, the combination of intelligent hydrogels with specific degradability and Fe–N–C nanozymes with enhanced activity in one system to achieve controllable and sensitive detection is rare. Herein, we combine intelligent hydrogel with mimic peroxidase activity enhanced Fe–N–C nanozymes to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensitive detection of hyaluronidase (HAase). The modification of copper ions has been proved to enhance the mimic enzyme activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes greatly. Cu2+ modified Fe–N–C nanozymes were embedded in hyaluronic acid hydrogel. In the presence of HAase, the HA hydrogel structure was hydrolyzed and released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes gradually. The released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes are used to catalyze the hydrogen peroxide system so that o-phenylenediamine is oxidized to orange fluorescent 2, 3-diaminophenolazine (DAP). Due to the electrostatic interaction, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer can occur between the negatively charged copper nanoclusters emitted by 430 nm and the positively charged DAP emitted by 560 nm. The activity of HAase was monitored according to the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 560 nm and 430 nm (F560/F430). The linear range of this method is 0–10.0 U/ml and the detection limit is 0.43 U/mL (S/N = 3). This strategy has been further applied to biological samples successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of promising artificial enzyme, nanozymes have been widely applied in analysis and bio-sensing [1]. Unlike natural enzymes with low stability, high cost, and difficulty in storage and recovery, nanozymes possess excellent properties of highly stable, superior activity, and economic expenditure [2]. Since ferroferric oxide nanoparticles were found to have mimic peroxidase activity in 2007 [3], more and more nanozymes, such as metal nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, MOFs and so on, have been discovered and employed in the applications of sensing, antibacterial agents, and therapy [4,5]. Recently, researchers are committed to precisely regulating the structure of nanozymes and studying their catalytic mechanism as specific mimic enzymes [6–8]. Meanwhile, exploring practical ways to enhance the activity of nanozymes is also an attractive research topic for the achievement of a more efficient catalytic reaction. Heteroatom doping and metal ion modification engineering as the general strategies have been widely used in the design of nanozymes to enhance their activities [9–12]. However, as a novel nanomaterial, the study on enhancing Fe–N–C nanozymes activity needs to be further investigated.

Many biological structures can respond to changes in temperature, humidity, light, magnetic fields and other surrounding environment, and produce intelligent responses, deformations and even drive behaviors. The specific intelligent structures endowed by nature to living organisms and their functional mechanisms of stress deformation and autonomous drive provide new ideas for the innovative functional design and preparation of intelligent hydrogels, such as switches, microrobots, artificial muscles and other biomimetic properties [13]. Due to its response to various external signal stimuli, intelligent hydrogels have been attached attention widely. Various signal responsive hydrogels, such as pH-response [14], photo-response [15], DNA-response [16], enzyme-response [17], ultrasound-response [18], and magnetic-response [19], have been applied in sensing and therapeutics. Benefit from the high specificity and degradability, enzyme-responsive hydrogels are widely used for targeted drug delivery and release [20]. Enzyme-controlled release specifically includes two methods: one is to contain chemical bonds in the hydrogel that are easily degraded by specific enzymes, and the breakage of the chemical bonds causes the release of the contents. The other is that the monomer of the hydrogel is easily degraded by a specific enzyme to release the contents. Different from the purpose of drug release, researchers have successfully designed a kind of controllable release sensors based on the use of enzyme-response hydrogels for the determination of analytes recently [21,22], thus the development of enzyme-responsive hydrogels in sensing is in line with academic expectations.

Hyaluronidase (HAase) is an endogenous glycosidase that degrades the glycosidic bonds in hyaluronic acid (HA) polymers [23]. As an essential glycoside hydrolase, HAase plays an essential role in regulating life activities. The study revealed that the activity of HAase in urine were related to prostate cancer closely [24]. In a recent study, HAase is also related to apoptosis and senescent [25]. Therefore, the determination of HAase in clinical diagnosis has gradually attract the attention of researchers. Many traditional methods have been presented for detecting HAase, including immunoassay [26], viscosimetry [27] and colorimetry [28]. However, some drawbacks need to be improved of the above approaches, such as lack of sensitivity and the necessity of the expensive antibodies. Due to the characteristics of simplicity and high sensitivity, the fluorescence method has received more attention from researchers. Several fluorescent biosensors have been reported for HAase assay [29–31]. However, most of these sensors are designed to take advantage of the negativity of HA based on the electrostatic interaction strategy, which is ignoring other available properties of HA. Therefore, exploring and applying other unique properties of HA to construct fluorescence sensors to detect HAase is undeniably attractive.

In this work, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes was greatly enhanced by modifying copper ion (Cu2+) for the first time. Cu2+ modified Fe–N–C nanozymes (Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes) were embedded in HA hydrogel. In the presence of HAase, the HA hydrogel structure was hydrolyzed and released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes gradually. Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes were collected to catalyze the hydrogen peroxide system, causing the oxidation of the non-fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2, 3-diaminophenolazine (DAP) with orange fluorescence. Meanwhile, the negatively charged PVP-stabilized copper nanoclusters (PVP–CuNCs) emitted at 430 nm, and the positively charged DAP were combined by electrostatic interaction and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurredScheme 1 ). With the increase of HAase activity, the fluorescence peak of DAP at 560 nm gradually increased, while the fluorescence peak of copper nanoclusters at 430 nm gradually quenched. The activity of HAase was monitored by the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 560 nm to that at 430 nm (F560/F430). The linear range of this method is 0–10.0 U/ml and the detection limit is 0.43 U/mL (S/N = 3). This strategy has been further applied to biological samples successfully. As far as we know, this is the first ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the combination of controlled release of intelligent hydrogel and enhanced mimic activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes to detect biomarkers.

Chemicals Hyaluronidase (HAase), sodium hyaluronate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Industrial Corporation. Glucosamine hydrochloride, 1-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), ascorbic acid (AA), polyethyleneimine (PEI), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Ferric chloride (FeCl3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2) were bought from Macklin Industrial Corporation. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt %), copper sulfate (CuSO4), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and other chemicals were bought from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. All of the reagents were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification. Ultrapure water produced by a Millipore system (USA) was utilized in all aqueous solutions.

Instruments The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Xenon lamp, and the slit widths were set to be 5.0 nm for both the excitation and emission. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Thermo Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Madison, USA). The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Thermo Scientific microplate reader (Shanghai, China). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was measured on a JEOL JEM-3010 microscope (Tokyo, Japan), operated at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV.

Synthesis of the Fe–N–C nanozymes Reference to the previous work [32], the mixture of 2.5 g FeCl3, ZnCl2, and glucosamine hydrochloride (mole ratio 1:2:10) was added in 2.0 g SiO2 suspension. After stirring for 5 min, the solution was freeze-dried overnight. The powder was then pyrolyzed with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 to 850 °C and kept for 2 h. The obtained powder was etched by hydrofluoric acid for 10 min. The resultant Fe–N–C nanozymes were freeze-dried overnight and stored at room temperature for further use.

Synthesis of the Cu
2+
−Fe–N–C nanozymes Reference to the previous work [33], Cu2+−Fe–N–C nanozymes were prepared by mixing Fe–N–C nanozymes and CuSO4 under sonication conditions. Briefly, 10.0 mg of Fe–N–C nanozymes were added in 10.0 mL H2O under sonication for 10 min. Then 10.0 mL of CuSO4 (0.1 M) was mixed with the above solution. After sonication treatment for 2 h, the solution was purified under the centrifugal and washed with ultrapure water three times to remove the impurities. Finally, the product was dried in vacuum at 70 °C overnight.

Synthesis of the PVP-CuNCs Reference to the previous work [34], 500.0 mg PVP was dissolved in 8.0 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min. Then 100.0 μL of 0.1 M CuSO4 was added in the above solution. After the mixture formed a homogeneous solution, 200.0 μL of 1 M AA was mixed with the solution and stirred for 24 h under 50 °C in water bath. Finally, the resultant light-yellow product was centrifuged by an ultrafiltration tube, and then stored at 4 °C for future use.

Fabrication of Cu
2+
−Fe–N–C nanozymes embedded HA hydrogel Briefly, 10.0 μL of 1 wt% HA was mixed with 20.0 μL of 30.0 mg/mL Cu2+−Fe–N–C nanozymes in centrifugal tube and shaken well for 10 min. Then 5.0 μL of 50.0 mg/mL EDC and 15.0 mg/mL NHS were added into the above mixture and shaken for 10 min to activate carboxylic group. After that, 10.0 μL of 0.025 wt% PEI was added in the solution to form chemical bonds with HA for 1 h. The obtained HA hydrogel was stored at room temperature for the later use.

Determination of HAase activity 60 μL of HAase with different activities ranging from 0 to 50.0 U/mL were added to the top of the hydrogel in centrifugal tube to hydrolyze HA at 37 °C. After the incubation for 2 h, 50.0 μL of supernatant was collected and added in 150.0 μL of mixture with 50.0 μL of 1 M H2O2, 50.0 μL of 10.0 mM OPD and 50.0 μL of PVP-CuNCs. The solution was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence spectrum is collected at the excitation wavelength of 351 nm.

Appropriate functionalization can provide nanomaterials with better functionality, such as electronegativity, specific functional groups, and so on [35]. Polymer-functionalized nanoclusters are known for their ability to create the desired surface functionalities, which can be widely used in sensing and biosensing [36]. The obtained PVP-CuNCs were characterized by TEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the synthesized PVP-CuNCs show uniform morphology and good dispersion, and their size was calculated to be about 2 nm (Fig. S1). The UV–vis absorption spectra of PVP-CuNCs (Fig. 1B) show that PVP-CuNCs has an absorption peak in the range of 300–310 nm, while no obvious surface plasmon resonance characteristic absorption peak of copper nanoparticles is detected at 585 nm [37], indicating that PVP-CuNCs has molecular-like properties and ultra-fine size, and there is no larger copper nanoparticles. The obtained PVP-CuNCs were excited at 351 nm, and the emission peak was observed at 430 nm. Interestingly, different from excitation independent emission in the Kasha-Vavilov rule, with the different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescent spectra of PVP-CuNCs perform different emission peaks (Fig. 1C). Because of the uneven size distribution of the PVP-CuNCs, this excitation-dependent property indicates that the PVP-CuNCs contain a quantized set of energy states, with each excitation wavelength corresponding to the emission of a specific energy level [38]. As presented in Fig. 1D, the IR characteristic peaks of PVP-CuNCs and PVP polymer performed consistent, except for the intensity of transmittance. This shows that PVP was successfully modified on the surface of CuNCs, and its structure remained stable even at high temperature [39].

The obtained Fe–N–C nanozymes were characterized by TEM, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), high angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF STEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM image (Fig. 2A) shows that the Fe–N–C nanozymes have typical nanosheets with drapes. HAADF STEM and EDS mapping (Fig. 2B) indicate that Fe, N, and C elements are distributed in Fe–N–C nanozymes structure uniformly. High-resolution N 1s analysis of XPS reveals that the existence of pyrrolic N, FeNx species, pyridinic N, oxidized N, and graphitic N (Fig. 2C) [40], this result proves the existence of Fe-Nx active sites [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes was studied by selecting OPD as the peroxidase substrate. As presented in Fig. S2, with the addition of H2O2, Fe–N–C-NZ catalyzed the oxidation of non-fluorescent OPD to DAP with an orange fluorescence emission peak at 560 nm. In contrast, in the absence of H2O2, there is almost no fluorescence in the system, and there is only a weak fluorescence emission peak in the absence of Fe–N–C nanozymes. Meanwhile, as one chromogenic substrate, colorless OPD can turn into typical yellow color after the oxidation reaction. Thus UV–vis spectra of the catalytic oxidation system containing OPD, H2O2 and Fe–N–C nanozymes were also obtained. As shown in Fig. 2D, the DAP in the catalytic oxidation system preformed a characteristic absorption peak at 418 nm with apparent yellow color. Moreover, other typical chromogenic substrate (TMB, PPD and ABTS) were used to further verify the Fe–N–C nanozymes peroxidase-like activity. In the presence of H2O2, Fe–N–C nanozymes also can catalyze the oxidation of TMB, PPD and ABTS to blue, brown and slight pink (Inset of Fig. 2D), and the characteristic absorptions are distributed at 654, 490, and 420 nm, respectively. These results indicated the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes.

To verify that the Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes have higher peroxidase mimic activity, the same mass fraction of Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes and Fe–N–C nanozymes were added to the H2O2 catalytic system under the same conditions, and the UV absorption spectra were collected at the same time. It can be seen from Fig. 2 E that in the reaction system added Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes, the absorption peak of DAP at 430 nm is obviously higher than that of adding Fe–N–C nanozymes. From the corresponding inset, it can also be seen that the color substrate of the reaction system added Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes is darker. To more directly reflect the difference between the two reaction rates, absorbance values of the reaction system were collected every 30 s. It was found that the catalytic reaction system reached equilibrium in about 10 min after the addition of Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes, while the absorbance of the reaction system with the addition of Fe–N–C nanozymes changed only a little (Fig. 2F). The above results show that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe–N–C nanozymes is greatly enhanced after copper ion modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FRET process of the purposed sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction mechanism between PVP-CuNCs and DAP was further investigated by using zeta potential, absorption spectra, and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the absorption spectrum of OPD presents no characteristic absorption peak, and the fluorescence emission spectrum of PVP-CuNCs performs a large overlap with the absorption spectrum of DAP. Meanwhile, the zeta potentials of PVP-CuNCs, DAP and PVP-CuNCs/DAP were measured to be −6.1 mV, 4.2 mV and −2.8 mV, respectively (Fig. 3B), which reveal that PVP-CuNCs and DAP can be combined by electrostatic interaction. These results indicate that the process of FRET can occur between PVP-CuNCs and DAP. The fluorescence quenching of PVP-CuNCs regulated by DAP was also observed via the effect of FRET. As shown in Fig. 3C, DAP and PVP-CuNCs perform fluorescence emission peaks at 560 and 430 nm, respectively. However, in the reaction system of PVP-CuNCs and DAP, the fluorescence intensity of DAP increased a certain, and the fluorescence of PVP-CuNCs was quenched obviously. To obtain the fluorescence change trend of the interaction between DAP and PVP more intuitively, the various concentration of DAP was reacted with PVP-CuNCs. As presented in Fig. 3D, with the increase of DAP concentration, the fluorescence intensity of PVP-CuNCs gradually decreased, and the fluorescence intensity of DAP maintained increasing.

To investigate the optimal experimental conditions for the detection of HAase, the enzymatic reaction time of the enzymatic reaction system of HA hydrogel at 37 °C, pH 7.4 and the reaction time of the hydrogen peroxide-catalyzed system were optimized. The suitable enzymatic digestion time can not only improve the detection sensitivity of the sensor, but also improve the detection efficiency of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when the enzymatic digestion time was in the range of 30–120 min, the HA hydrogel was rapidly hydrolyzed by HAase to release Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes, which made the value of the system F560/F430 gradually increase. When the enzymatic reaction was complete after 120 min, the value of F560/F430 increased slowly and stabilized. In order to ensure the normal reaction and detection efficiency, 120 min was chosen as the optimal enzymatic digestion time. Similarly, the time for Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes to catalyze the production of oxygen from H2O2 can also affect the performance of this sensor. A suitable catalytic time can ensure the detection effectiveness of the sensor as well as improve the detection efficiency. As shown in Fig. 4B, the F560/F430 value of the reaction system increases rapidly when the catalytic time is in the range of 10–30 min. When the catalytic time is after 30 min, the catalytic reaction tends to be smooth and the F560/F430 value of the reaction system gradually stabilizes. Therefore, 30 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for the H2O2 catalytic system.

The possibly potential biological species was used to evaluate the specificity of the constructed platform for HAase activity determination, including glucose oxidase (GOx), lysozyme (LYS), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), human serum albumin (HSA), β-galactosidase (β-Gal), NaCl and KCl in the buffer solution (Tris-HCl, pH 7.4) were chosen as presented in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence ratio (F/F0) of the sensing system can be enhanced by the addition of ALP and HSA slightly, indicating that interfering substances led to almost no fluorescence change for this established platform.

To evaluate the performance of the proposed platform, HAase in healthy human urine was analyzed by the constructed system. As shown in Table 2 , after spiking the samples with different activities of HAase, a good recovery varying from 94.8% to 107.8% with the RSD ranging from 3.74% to 6.03% were obtained in this platform. The measured data are compared with the determination of HAase activity ELISA kit, and obtained the results with no significant difference. These results indicate this strategy can be used for the determination of HAase activity in biological matrices.

In summary, a mimic-enzyme activity enhancement and controllable release strategy for sensitive detection of HAase has been proposed, which is based on the combination of Cu2+ modified Fe–N–C nanozymes and degradable intelligent hydrogel. The synthesized Fe–N–C nanozymes possess peroxidase-like activity, and the mimic-enzyme activity has been enhanced by Cu2+. Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes were embedded in HA hydrogel. In the presence of HAase, the structure of HA hydrogel was hydrolyzed and released Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes gradually. Cu2+-Fe-N-C nanozymes were collected to catalyze the H2O2 system, causing the oxidation of the non-fluorescent OPD to DAP with orange fluorescence. Meanwhile, the negatively charged PVP-CuNCs emitted at 430 nm and the positively charged DAP were combined by electrostatic interaction, and the FRET occurred. With the increase of HAase activity, the fluorescence peak of DAP at 560 nm gradually increased, while the fluorescence peak of PVP-CuNCs at 430 nm gradually quenched. The activity of HAase was monitored by the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 560 nm to that at 430 nm (F560/F430) and obtain the satisfactory results. Benefits from the efficient controllable release and mimic activity enhancement, this sensor presents high sensitivity and practicality, which offers guidance to design multiple strategies in one system to construct more attractive and facile fluorescent sensors.

Xuemin Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Qingfeng Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Cuimin Wu: Supervision, Zhenyu Lin: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Aiwen Huang: Supervision. Methodology. Bin Qiu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of STS Key Project of Fujian Province (2017T3007), Nature Sciences Funding of Fujian Province (2018J01682).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122961.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics (nanozymes): next-generation artificial enzymes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wei, E. Wang, Nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics (nanozymes): next-generation artificial enzymes, Chemical Society reviews 42 (2013) 6060-6093.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanozyme: an emerging alternative to natural enzyme for biosensing and immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, H. Wei, Z. Zhang, E. Wang, S. Dong, Nanozyme: An emerging alternative to natural enzyme for biosensing and immunoassay, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry 105 (2018) 218-224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metallic nanomaterials with mimic oxidoreductase enzyme activity: new insight for sensing and biosensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanozymes: classification, catalytic mechanisms, activity regulation, and applications
